source_paper_title: Finding Hall Blockers by Matrix Scaling
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Hayashi_2024, title={Finding Hall Blockers by Matrix Scaling}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0198}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0198}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Hayashi, Koyo and Hirai, Hiroshi and Sakabe, Keiya}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2166–2179} }
context:
Page 3, Introduction (discussion of operator scaling extension and problem raised in prior work)

It is a natural question whether the operator Sinkhorn algorithm can find a Hall blocker for nonscalable case. This may need to characterize the limits of the operator Sinkhorn algorithm, which is raised as a problem in [18].
title: Characterize and compute limits of operator Sinkhorn algorithm for nonscalable operators
background_latex:
Let \(\Phi\) be a completely positive linear operator acting on complex matrices (equivalently, a linear map \(\Phi: \mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\to\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\) that maps positive semidefinite matrices to positive semidefinite matrices). Operator scaling asks for invertible matrices \(L,R\) such that the scaled operator \(X\mapsto L\,\Phi(RXR^*)\,L^*\) satisfies prescribed left and right marginals (the operator analogue of making a nonnegative matrix doubly stochastic). The operator Sinkhorn algorithm (also called the Gurvits algorithm) is an iterative normalization procedure that alternately rescales the operator to correct its left and right marginals, generalizing the classical Sinkhorn algorithm for matrix scaling.

In the nonscalable case (i.e., when no choice of \(L,R\) can make \(\Phi\) doubly stochastic, even approximately), an analogue of a Hall-type certificate exists: a nontrivial subspace \(W\subsetneq \mathbb{C}^n\) that is "shrunk" by \(\Phi\) (often called a shrunk subspace). Existence of such a shrunk subspace certifies nonscalability, in the same way that a Hall blocker certifies absence of a perfect matching for bipartite graphs.

The cited paper discusses extending matrix-scaling-based certificate-finding results to operator scaling, and notes that doing so may require understanding the limiting behavior (or limit objects/accumulation structure) of the operator Sinkhorn iterates in the nonscalable case.
problem_definition_latex: Determine (i.e., characterize and provide a method to compute) the limits or accumulation-point structure of the iterates produced by the operator Sinkhorn algorithm when applied to a completely positive operator \(\Phi\) that is not (approximately) scalable to doubly stochastic form.
latex:
Let \(\Phi\) be a completely positive linear operator acting on complex matrices (equivalently, a linear map \(\Phi: \mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\to\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\) that maps positive semidefinite matrices to positive semidefinite matrices). The operator Sinkhorn algorithm (also called the Gurvits algorithm) is an iterative normalization procedure that alternately rescales the operator to correct its left and right marginals, generalizing the classical Sinkhorn algorithm for matrix scaling.

In the nonscalable case (i.e., when no choice of \(L,R\) can make \(\Phi\) doubly stochastic, even approximately), an analogue of a Hall-type certificate exists: a nontrivial subspace \(W\subsetneq \mathbb{C}^n\) that is "shrunk" by \(\Phi\) (often called a shrunk subspace). Existence of such a shrunk subspace certifies nonscalability, in the same way that a Hall blocker certifies absence of a perfect matching for bipartite graphs.

Determine (i.e., characterize and provide a method to compute) the limits or accumulation-point structure of the iterates produced by the operator Sinkhorn algorithm when applied to a completely positive operator \(\Phi\) that is not (approximately) scalable to doubly stochastic form.
location_in_paper: Page 3, Introduction (discussion of operator scaling extension and problem raised in prior work)
open_problem_quote: It is a natural question whether the operator Sinkhorn algorithm can find a Hall blocker for nonscalable case. This may need to characterize the limits of the operator Sinkhorn algorithm, which is raised as a problem in [18].
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem asks for a general characterization of limiting behavior for all nonscalable completely positive operators, not a bounded finite-family statement where a single small instance would refute a conjecture. While one can simulate iterates numerically, a counterexample would not resolve the characterization question without a matching theory framework.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can programmatically represent completely positive operators (e.g., via Kraus operators) and simulate operator Sinkhorn iterations, using empirical convergence/oscillation diagnostics as a fitness signal to hypothesize structural patterns. However, translating discovered patterns into a correct general characterization is nontrivial and likely requires substantial theoretical work beyond automated search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for a characterization and computable description of the limit/accumulation structure of the operator Sinkhorn (Gurvits) iterates when a completely positive operator \(\Phi\) is not (approximately) scalable to prescribed marginals (in particular, doubly stochastic). In the commutative matrix case, the source paper (Hayashi–Hirai–Sakabe) builds on the information-geometric view of Sinkhorn as alternating KL-projections (Csisz\'ar–Tusn\'ady; Gietl–Reffel) and on Aas’s analysis of nonscalable limits to show that the iterates approach a structured oscillatory limit whose block decomposition is governed by extreme stable sets / principal partitions / refined Dulmage–Mendelsohn decompositions. This structure can be computed (via parametric flow) and then used to extract Hall blockers (certificates of nonscalability) from sufficiently advanced iterates.

For operator scaling, the scalable case is well understood: capacity serves as a potential, and the operator Sinkhorn algorithm decides (approximate) scalability in polynomial time (Garg–Gurvits–Oliveira–Wigderson), building on Gurvits’s original formulation. In the nonscalable case, shrunk subspaces play the role of Hall blockers, but the detailed asymptotic behavior of the unmodified operator Sinkhorn iterates remains largely uncharacterized; surveys such as Garg–Oliveira explicitly flag this as an open direction. A promising route is to develop a noncommutative analogue of the KL-divergence alternating-minimization picture that underlies the matrix nonscalable-limit theory; Matsuda–Soma makes progress by developing an information-geometric framework for operator scaling that could support such a characterization.

Algorithmically, Franks–Soma–Goemans provide strong partial progress: they modify the operator Sinkhorn iteration/potential framework to output a shrunk subspace in polynomial time when \(\Phi\) is nonscalable, thereby obtaining certificates without needing a full description of the original iterates’ accumulation set. However, this does not settle the specific question of describing the limit objects (e.g., whether there is a two-cycle/oscillation phenomenon, a canonical block decomposition, or a variational characterization of accumulation points) for the standard operator Sinkhorn algorithm in the nonscalable regime. Establishing such a theory would likely require identifying the correct noncommutative divergence/projection geometry and relating the resulting limit structure to invariant-theoretic obstructions (shrunk subspaces) in a way analogous to how principal partitions control the matrix case.

sources.title: Operator scaling: theory and applications
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops the theory of operator scaling for completely positive maps, including the operator Sinkhorn (Gurvits) algorithm, capacity as a potential function, and a polynomial-time algorithm to decide (approximate) scalability to doubly stochastic form (and more general marginals).
sources.connection: Provides the baseline convergence/complexity theory in the scalable case and frames the nonscalable case via shrunk subspaces; the open problem asks for a characterization of the limiting/accumulation behavior of the same iterates when scalability fails.

sources.title: Shrunk subspaces via operator Sinkhorn iteration
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives a polynomial-time method, based on a modified operator Sinkhorn iteration/potential framework, to output a shrunk subspace certificate when an operator is not scalable (to specified marginals).
sources.connection: Circumvents the need to fully characterize accumulation points of the unmodified operator Sinkhorn iterates by modifying the procedure to extract a shrunk subspace; this is strong partial progress toward algorithmic certification in the nonscalable regime, but does not by itself characterize the original iterates' limit set.

sources.title: Information geometry of operator scaling
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops an information-geometric viewpoint for operator scaling, introducing noncommutative analogues of divergence and geometric structures that relate scaling steps to projections/alternating minimization in a suitable geometry.
sources.connection: The matrix case’s nonscalable-limit analysis in the source paper relies on KL-divergence alternating minimization and limit oscillation structure; this work is a key step toward an operator analogue of that divergence framework, potentially enabling characterization of accumulation points in the nonscalable operator case.

sources.title: Classical complexity and quantum entanglement
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces operator scaling (in the context of quantum entanglement) and the Gurvits/operator Sinkhorn algorithm, connecting progress to capacity-like quantities and invariant theory.
sources.connection: Foundational source for the operator Sinkhorn iteration whose nonscalable limiting behavior is being asked to characterize; also motivates shrunk subspaces as obstructions.

sources.title: Operator scaling with specified marginals
sources.theorem_or_result: Extends operator scaling to prescribed marginals and studies algorithmic/structural aspects of scaling completely positive maps beyond the doubly stochastic setting.
sources.connection: The open problem concerns nonscalable operators for prescribed left/right marginals; this paper provides the formalism and conditions in which the operator Sinkhorn iteration is run and analyzed.

sources.title: Recent progress on scaling algorithms and applications
sources.theorem_or_result: Survey of matrix and operator scaling, including algorithmic results, invariant-theoretic viewpoints, and open problems about convergence and certificates.
sources.connection: Explicitly highlights the open issue of understanding the operator Sinkhorn algorithm’s behavior in the nonscalable regime, aligning with the problem statement here.

sources.title: Finding Hall blockers by matrix scaling
sources.theorem_or_result: Characterizes nonscalable matrix Sinkhorn accumulation via KL-divergence alternating minimization, yielding a block-triangular/DM-type decomposition and polynomial-time extraction of (parametric) Hall blockers from iterates.
sources.connection: Provides the matrix analogue and methodological template (divergence minimization, limit block structure, extraction of certificates) that the open operator-scaling problem seeks to generalize to completely positive maps.

sources.title: Limit points of the iterative scaling procedure
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes accumulation/limit points of iterative proportional fitting (Sinkhorn) in the nonscalable matrix case, proving an oscillatory two-cycle structure and a block decomposition computable in polynomial time.
sources.connection: This is the key precedent for nonscalable-limit characterization in the commutative (matrix) setting; it motivates seeking an operator analogue of oscillation/block structure for completely positive maps.

sources.title: Accumulation points of the iterative proportional fitting procedure
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows that in the nonscalable matrix case, Sinkhorn/IPFP does not converge but oscillates between two matrices that minimize a KL-divergence between row- and column-normalized affine sets, using information-geometric alternating minimization.
sources.connection: Supplies the divergence-minimization lens used in the source paper; an operator analogue of this KL-projection picture is a natural route to characterizing operator Sinkhorn accumulation points.
